This is a very big category of spending right now, in which there are a lot of whales. So you gotta think through in your business category, in your business, who might be the whales, what might be a whale for you, what criteria you wanna establish for the whale, you wanna now deliberately go get. Just as example, I'll tell you my. Clients, not GKIC members now, but clients, A, I need somebody who will invest upwards from $75,000 on a project. And that rules out a whole lot of people. Not just because, as Barbara accused me of, their cheapskates, it's the size and scope of their opportunity doesn't warrant spending that kind of money. Doesn't mean they're wrong at all, just means they're wrong for me. They might be a great client for somebody else, they're just not a good client for me, which gets to, they have a sufficient size and scope and growth potential of their opportunity. So one way or another, they can give me the compensation I want and there's likelihood of year to year, to year to year continuation of a relationship. They have to be reasonably intelligent and capable. So the number one way that freelance copywriters don't get their royalties is the client can't implement the work after they get it. The number two way freelance copywriters don't get their royalties is the client can't implement the number three way freelance copywriters don't get their royalties is the client can't. So they have to be relatively intelligent and capable, or to me, they're not particularly valuable because I don't just get money on the front, I get money on the back, they have to be respectful, well-behaved and low maintenance. Now for somebody else again, but not for me. So a high maintenance client's no good for me. So they have to, evidence that they can be trained to be high maintenance with everybody else if they want to but be low maintenance with Dan, or that's not gonna work. They have to be a leader or a leader in the making in a field so they're useful to me in catching another whale. I do want, I don't need a.
